questions.question: Explain the dual-mode operation of an Operating System. Why is it necessary for system security?
keyPoints: Defines two modes: user mode and kernel mode
User mode has limited privileges, kernel mode has full privileges
Prevents user programs from executing privileged instructions
Protects critical system resources from unauthorized access
Enables protection and isolation between processes

questions.question: Describe the sequence of events during the computer boot process.
keyPoints: Power on and BIOS/UEFI initialization
POST (Power-On Self Test) diagnostic
Bootstrap program location and execution
Loading of OS kernel into memory
Initialization of system services and devices
Display of login prompt

questions.question: What are system calls? Explain their role in providing OS services to user programs.
keyPoints: System calls are programming interfaces to OS kernel services
Provide process control, file management, device communication
Act as bridge between user programs and hardware
Include functions like open, read, write, fork, exec
User programs cannot directly access hardware resources

questions.question: Explain the concept of interrupts. How does the CPU handle interrupt-driven I/O?
keyPoints: Interrupts are signals requiring immediate CPU attention
CPU saves current execution state
Looks up interrupt vector for handler address
Executes appropriate interrupt handler
Restores state and resumes normal execution
Allows efficient handling of asynchronous events

questions.question: What is Direct Memory Access (DMA)? How does it improve system performance?
keyPoints: DMA allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory
Reduces CPU involvement in data transfer
CPU can perform other computations during I/O
Controller manages data transfer autonomously
Improves overall system throughput and efficiency
Commonly used by disk drives, sound cards, network cards

questions.question: Explain the storage hierarchy in computer systems with examples of each level.
keyPoints: CPU Registers: fastest, smallest capacity (e.g., accumulator)
Cache: fast, small, between CPU and memory
Main Memory (RAM): primary working memory
Magnetic Disk: secondary storage, larger capacity
Tape/Optical Storage: archival, slowest
Shows trade-off between speed, cost, and capacity

questions.question: Describe the functions of an Operating System as a resource manager.
keyPoints: Manages CPU through process scheduling
Controls memory allocation and deallocation
Manages I/O device allocation and scheduling
Handles file system operations
Coordinates access to shared resources
Ensures fair and efficient resource distribution

questions.question: Explain the role of an Operating System as an intermediary between the user and computer hardware. Discuss both the user view and system view of an OS.
keyPoints: OS as Extended Machine: hides hardware complexity from users
OS as Resource Manager: allocates and controls hardware resources
User View: focuses on convenience, ease of use, performance, simplicity
System View: focuses on efficient resource management and protection
Examples of abstraction: file systems, device drivers, process management
Balancing act between user convenience and system efficiency

questions.question: Explain the concept of interrupt-driven I/O in detail. Discuss different types of interrupts and how the interrupt handling mechanism works.
keyPoints: Interrupt types: hardware (external), software (internal), exceptions
Interrupt Vector: table of handler addresses
Handling steps: detection, save state, vector lookup, handler execution, restore state
Polling vs interrupt-driven approaches
Priority levels and interrupt nesting
Real-time considerations and interrupt latency
DMA role in reducing interrupt frequency

questions.question: Discuss the bootstrap process in detail. What are the key components involved, and what happens at each stage from power-on to login prompt?
keyPoints: ROM/EEPROM stores bootstrap program
BIOS/UEFI initializes basic hardware components
POST tests memory and essential hardware
BIOS searches for bootable device (disk, USB, network)
Loads first sector (boot loader) into memory
Boot loader loads OS kernel from boot device
Kernel initializes: sets up memory management, process scheduling
Device drivers loaded and hardware initialized
System services (daemons) started
Login screen or command prompt displayed

questions.question: What is protection in operating systems? Explain the mechanisms used to ensure protection and security in modern operating systems.
keyPoints: Protection: controlling access to system resources
Dual-mode operation separates user/kernel modes
Privileged instructions only executable in kernel mode
Memory protection: base/limit registers, MMU
CPU protection: timer prevents infinite loops
System calls provide controlled access to kernel services
User IDs and permissions for file/process access
Additional mechanisms: authentication, encryption, firewalls
